title: Angelica sinensis (Dong Quai)
medicine: Dong quai (Angelica sinensis) has many health benefits, such as lowering cholesterol levels, decreasing inflammation, and boosting energy. This plant is also known to improve digestion and alleviate symptoms of arthritis. Additionally, dong quai can help with menopause symptoms, including hot flashes and mood swings. It has also been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries as a remedy for colds and flu.

The medicinal properties of dong quai are attributed to its various constituents, such as vitamins, minerals, and phytonutrients. Some of the most important compounds found in dong quai include ferulic acid, shogaol, and polysaccharides. Ferulic acid is an antioxidant that helps protect against damage from free radicals, while shogaol has anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of arthritis. The polysaccharides in dong quai are believed to boost the immune system and improve digestion.

The most commonly used parts of dong quai include the root, stem, and leaves. Dong quai root is often used to make tea or supplements, while the stem can be eaten raw or cooked like a vegetable. The leaves are also edible and can be used in salads or as a garnish. In traditional Chinese medicine, dong quai is commonly used in the form of capsules, tablets, or teas.

While dong quai is generally considered safe for most people, it may cause side effects in some individuals. These side effects can include stomach upset, diarrhea, and headaches. In rare cases, dong quai may interact with certain medications, so it is important to consult with a healthcare provider before taking it. Pregnant women should also avoid using dong quai, as it may increase the risk of bleeding.

To ensure the safety and effectiveness of dong quai, it is important to purchase it from reputable sources. Look for products that are standardized for ferulic acid content and have been tested for heavy metals and other contaminants. It is also important to follow the recommended dosage and consult with a healthcare provider before taking dong quai if you have any underlying health conditions or are taking medications.
horticulture: Dong Quai (Angelica sinensis) is a perennial herb that grows to a height of 60-80 cm and has a spreading habit. It requires full sun or partial shade and well-drained soil with a pH of 5.5-7.0. The plant has blue-green leaves that are lanceolate in shape and grow up to 30 cm long.

To propagate Dong Quai, seeds can be sown directly into the ground in late spring or early summer. Planting depth is about 1-2 cm and spacing should be around 30-45 cm between plants and rows. Keep the soil moist during germination and early growth.

Dong Quai requires minimal care once it has established. It does not require fertilization, but can benefit from compost or well-rotted manure in late spring or early summer. Prune back any dead or dying branches to encourage bushy growth. Water thoroughly during dry spells.

Dong Quai can be harvested starting from the third year. Cut the stems close to the ground and allow them to dry for several days before grinding them into a powder or extract. The best time to harvest is in the fall when the leaves are fully mature.

Pests that may affect Dong Quai include aphids, spider mites, and whiteflies. These can be controlled with insecticidal soap or neem oil. Diseases that may affect the plant include root rot and leaf spot. To prevent root rot, ensure good drainage and keep soil moist but not waterlogged. Leaf spot can be prevented by providing good air circulation and removing infected leaves promptly.
botany: Dong Quai, with botanical name Angelica sinensis, belongs to the domain Eukarya, kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Rosales, family Araliaceae, genus Angelica, and species sinensis. It is commonly known as Chinese angelica or dong quai. This perennial herbaceous plant grows up to 1.5 meters tall and has a stem that is cylindrical, hairy, and sturdy. The leaves are pinnate with three leaflets each, which are lanceolate in shape and have serrated edges. The flowers are white or pink, with five petals, and bloom from July to September.

Dong Quai has a distinctive odor and taste, similar to licorice. Its roots contain compounds such as polysaccharides, flavonoids, and triterpenes, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is used in traditional Chinese medicine to treat various conditions, including menopause, inflammation, and digestive issues.

There are several variants of Dong Quai, such as Angelica chinensis and Angelica gigantea, which differ in their size and shape. Angelica chinensis is a smaller plant with thinner stems and leaves, while Angelica gigantea is a larger plant with thicker stems and leaves. These variants are used for different medicinal purposes and have different taste profiles.

Dong Quai is native to Northeast China and Korea and grows in damp areas with full sun exposure. It prefers well-drained soil and can tolerate partial shade. The plant is also cultivated commercially in other parts of Asia, including Japan, China, and Korea.

Dong Quai has a life cycle that consists of seeds, which are produced by the flowers. The seeds are oval in shape and have a grayish-brown color. They can be germinated in water or soil and take about two weeks to sprout. The plant grows from the roots and forms rosettes before sending up the stem. It takes about three years for the plant to mature and start flowering.
intro: Centuries of traditional Chinese medicine have praised Dong Quai, or Angelica sinensis, for its multifaceted health benefits. Often referred to as 'female ginseng', this herbal treasure promotes blood circulation, alleviating menstrual discomfort and regulating hormonal imbalances in women. Its potent antioxidant properties nourish the body internally, aiding recovery from fatigue and stress. Dong Quai also possesses immune-boosting capabilities, fortifying the body's defenses against illness. Furthermore, it assists in digestion, relieving stomach ailments, and supports liver health by detoxifying and clearing stagnation.

Cultivating Angelica sinensis requires specific horticultural conditions that mirror its native habitat in eastern Asia to preserve its medicinal properties. These plants thrive in cooler climates with abundant moisture but must be carefully placed as they are sensitive to excessive sunlight and extreme temperatures. Well-drained, fertile soil, rich in organic matter, is essential to their growth, and the soil pH should ideally fall between 6 and 7. Dong Quai seeds demand particular stratification, a process of cold treatment before germination, replicating the natural cycle of winter dormancy. The plant's cultivation also benefits from frequent dividing during the growing season to encourage new growth and maintain vigor.

Angelica sinensis, the botanical source of Dong Quai, is a perennial herb distinguished by its robust stature, reaching up to three meters tall, adorned with thick stems and deeply lobed, aromatic, dense green leaves. The plant's primary appeal lies in its branching umbels of tiny white flowers, later replaced by oblong or oval seed capsules. Its roots, renowned for their medicinal value, are fleshy and pale brownish-yellow, with a sweetish taste. These root systems are carefully harvested once every three to five years to preserve the plant's health and ensure the continuation of its therapeutic offerings.
